articles.topic: Pregnant women's mindset shapes postpartum sleep
articles.headline: Your Expectations About Sleep After Birth Might Predict Everything
articles.body: A study of 432 pregnant women found that 70% expected poor sleep postpartum—and here's the kicker: those expectations actually predicted worse sleep outcomes, even after accounting for prior sleep disorders and psychiatric history. The research suggests that what you believe about sleep during pregnancy matters more than you'd think. For new mothers anticipating the worst, postpartum anxiety made things even harder. The takeaway: addressing sleep attitudes and anxiety during prenatal care could prevent postpartum sleep disorders before they start.
articles.takeaway: Your beliefs about postpartum sleep matter. Addressing anxiety and negative expectations during pregnancy may improve actual sleep outcomes after delivery.

articles.topic: Menopause linked to brain structure changes
articles.headline: Menopause Quietly Reshapes Your Brain—Here's What Happens
articles.body: <cite index="49-2,49-3">A major study suggests menopause is linked to changes in brain structure, mental health, and sleep. Brain scans revealed grey matter loss in areas tied to memory and emotional regulation, while many women reported increased anxiety, depression, and fatigue.</cite> Researchers analyzed brain scans from nearly 125,000 women and found <cite index="51-7">differences in the hippocampus (responsible for forming and storing memories); entorhinal cortex (the 'gateway' for passing information between the hippocampus and the rest of the brain); and the anterior cingulate cortex (part of the brain that helps you manage emotions, make decisions, and focus your attention).</cite> <cite index="49-4">Hormone therapy did not reverse these effects, though it may slow age-related declines in reaction speed.</cite>
articles.takeaway: Menopause brings measurable brain changes in memory and emotion regions. These aren't prevented by hormone therapy, but recognizing the transition as a critical brain health moment matters.

product.name: Thorne Vitamin D3/K2
product.blurb: Most adults are deficient. D3 with K2 is the combination that actually works.

articles.topic: AI wearables detect stress before you feel it
articles.headline: Your Wearable Can Spot Stress Coming Before Your Brain Does
articles.body: AI algorithms in 2026 wearables are getting genuinely sophisticated. Instead of just counting steps, <cite index="59-8">they're detecting precursors to illness or stress before symptoms emerge, giving users and healthcare professionals valuable early warnings.</cite> <cite index="59-2,59-7">In 2026, it integrates input from a wide range of miniaturized sensors: tracking heart rate variability, respiration, blood oxygen levels, and more.</cite> The real power? <cite index="59-9">Instead of bombarding you with raw numbers, these devices contextualize trends, translating them into step-by-step guidance you can act on right away.</cite> It's the difference between data and actionable insight—finally.
articles.takeaway: Real wearables now synthesize multiple biometrics through AI to catch stress and early illness signs. The value is in the interpretation, not just the collection.

product.name: Garmin Forerunner 255
product.blurb: The watch Chris trains with daily. Tracks every discipline without breaking the bank.

articles.topic: Midlife exercise cuts dementia risk 45 percent
articles.headline: The Workout You Do Now Could Save Your Brain Later
articles.body: <cite index="79-7">Physical activity is among modifiable factors for dementia prevention, especially during midlife.</cite> A study tracking adults across their lifespan found that staying active in your 40s and 50s has outsized power for brain health in your 70s. <cite index="79-9">Past research has shown that being physically active in midlife or later life is associated with better brain structure, improved cognitive performance, and a lower risk of dementia.</cite> The key insight: midlife matters. The exercise doesn't have to be extreme—consistency beats intensity when it comes to long-term cognitive protection.
articles.takeaway: Exercise during midlife (40s–50s) may reduce dementia risk by up to 45%. The timing of your training, not just the intensity, shapes your brain's future.

product.name: Saucony Endorphin Speed 5
product.blurb: The shoe Chris trains and races in. Fast, light, built for people who take running seriously.

quote.text: You don't rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems.
quote.author: James Clear
quote.context: Author of Atomic Habits and behavioral science researcher focused on habit formation and continuous improvement.

workout.title: Short Stride Acceleration: 30/30 Intro to Speed
workout.tip: Focus on smooth arm drive and controlled breathing during the 30-second push—don't sprint all-out. Your recovery jog should feel easy; if you can't talk, you're recovering too hard.
workout.warmup: 5 min: easy jog at conversational pace, then 6 x 20m strides with 40m walk recovery between each (stride should feel controlled, not maximal—build into it)

exercises.name: 30/30 Interval Block
exercises.notes: Run at a pace you could sustain for about 3-4 minutes (roughly 85–90% max effort). Keep your head still, shoulders relaxed, and cadence quick. Recovery jog should feel genuinely easy—this teaches your body pacing discipline.

workout.cooldown: 3 min easy jog, then 2 min stationary walk. Standing quad stretch 20 sec each leg, standing hamstring reach 20 sec each leg, open hip stretch 15 sec each side.
workout.description: Your first exposure to running speed work using safe, short 30-second efforts followed by 30-second recovery jogs. This session teaches your nervous system to move faster without the injury risk of longer intervals, making it perfect for 5K and 10K beginners building speed foundations.

meal.title: Turkey Sandwich with Mustard & White Bread
meal.description: A simple, portable race-day fuel option for triathletes who prefer something savory. Easy to digest 2–3 hours before competition, with lean protein and quick carbs.
meal.instructions:
1. Spread mustard and honey evenly on two slices of bread.
2. Layer turkey slices between the bread.
3. Cut into triangles if desired for easier eating.
4. Eat 2–3 hours before race start with water on the side.
meal.tip: Buy pre-sliced turkey from the deli counter (not packaged) for better quality and less sodium if GI sensitivity is a concern. Store in a cool bag for morning transport.
